claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I can help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit. Let me ask you some questions to determine this.

**First, let's start with information about your child:**

What is the age of the child you're claiming for? 

- Are they **under 16** years old?
- Are they **16 to 19** years old?
- Or are they **20 or over**?

Please let me know your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, feel free to mention them all.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I've got two kids I'm asking about - Alex who's 6 years old, and Blake who's 3. So yes, they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children:
- **Alex**, who is 6 years old
- **Blake**, who is 3 years old

Both are under 16, which is great. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since both Alex (6) and Blake (3) are under 16, they automatically qualify in terms of age. 

Now, the next question (though it's more relevant for older children, but I need to check for completeness):

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given their ages (6 and 3), I'm assuming they're not in paid work, but please confirm so we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Ha, no, they're definitely not working! They're just little kids - Alex is in primary school and Blake is still in nursery. No jobs for them!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Ha ha, absolutely! That makes perfect sense - they're way too young to be working. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Again, given that they're only 6 and 3 years old, they're very unlikely to be claiming benefits themselves, but I need to confirm this to proceed.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't get any benefits in their own names. They're just kids - I'm the one who handles all that sort of stuff for the family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Let me continue with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! The next check is about apprenticeships:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeships?**

Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 3, they're definitely not in apprenticeships (those are typically for teenagers 16+), but I need to confirm this to move forward.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! They're way too young for apprenticeships. Like I said, Alex is just in primary school and Blake's in nursery. They're nowhere near that age yet!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! They're far too young for that. Let me move us forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we're moving on to questions about **you as the claimant**. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll meet this requirement if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with pre-settled status
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Do any of these apply to you? Most commonly, people simply normally live in the UK - is that your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester, so definitely here in the country.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now the next important question:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children**
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit toward their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do you contribute to their upkeep in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me. We all live together in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You live with both Alex and Blake, so you definitely meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Is another parent or guardian claiming for them?
- Is anyone else receiving Child Benefit payments for either child?

If there is someone else claiming, have you agreed with them that you should be the one to claim instead?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them and I haven't claimed it yet - that's why I'm asking now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, we can continue. Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check the children's living situation:

**Which of the following applies to Alex and Blake:**

- Are either of them currently **in hospital**?
- Are either of them **in care** (looked after by the local council)?
- Are either of them **fostered**?
- Or are they **living normally at home** with you?

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're both living at home with you in Manchester, but please confirm which situation applies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally. But Blake... he's actually been in hospital for a few months now. He's been there for quite a while - it's been tough.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm really sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital - that must be very difficult for you and your family. Thank you for sharing that.

Let me handle each child's situation separately since they're in different circumstances.

**For Alex (6 years old, living at home normally):**
Let me continue with Alex's assessment first.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:**
Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance? 

In other words, is the council paying for Alex's living costs or upkeep?

**For Blake (in hospital):**
I'll need to ask you separately about Blake's hospital stay. Can you tell me approximately how long Blake has been in hospital? Has it been:
- Less than 12 weeks?
- 12 weeks or more?

This will help me determine the eligibility rules that apply to Blake's situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - no, the council isn't paying anything for him. He just lives at home with me and I look after him.

For Blake - he's been in hospital for a few months now, so yes, it's been more than 12 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

**For Alex:** Great - since the council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, let me check his outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news for Alex! Now let me check Blake's situation. Since Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, I need to navigate through his assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now for Blake's hospital situation, let me navigate through that path:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask you an important question about Blake's hospital stay. Since Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, I need to check:

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital?**

This means you're continuing to pay for things for Blake such as:
- Clothes
- Toys or presents
- Personal items
- Travel costs to visit him
- Any other costs related to his care

Are you regularly spending money on Blake during his hospital stay?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on his needs while he's in there. It's been really hard financially with everything going on.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand - this must be an incredibly difficult time for you and your family, both emotionally and financially. Thank you for being honest about your situation.

Let me continue with Blake's assessment based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now I have all the information I need to provide you with a complete assessment for both children. Let me give you the final determination:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here are the results:

---

### **✅ ALEX (6 years old) - ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Living at home with you in Manchester
- Not in paid work or apprenticeship
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- You meet the UK residency requirements
- The local council is not paying for Alex's care
- No one else is claiming for Alex

**Next steps for Alex:**
1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. Provide proof of identity for yourself and Alex
3. Provide Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate
4. **Important note:** Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or your partner earns over £60,000
5. Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

### **❌ BLAKE (3 years old) - INELIGIBLE (current circumstances)**

Unfortunately, under the current circumstances, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

**Why:** Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks. Child Benefit normally continues for up to 12 weeks when a child is in hospital, but it stops after that **unless** you are regularly spending money on the child (such as for clothes, toys, personal items, travel costs to visit, etc.). Since you've indicated that you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while he's in hospital, the exception doesn't apply.

**Important information:**
- If your financial situation changes and you start regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital, you may become eligible again
- If Blake returns home, you can claim Child Benefit again
- If you have a partner (married or civil partnered and living together), their spending on Blake would also count
- Contact HMRC to check if any other exceptions might apply to your specific situation

**For more information:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-for-children-in-hospital-or-care

---

I'm truly sorry about Blake's hospital stay and the financial difficulties you're facing. This is clearly an extremely challenging time for your family. While you can proceed with claiming for Alex, please do contact HMRC directly about Blake's situation - they may be able to provide additional guidance or support options for your specific circumstances.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about these results?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all the eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), (6) You have responsibility for Alex as you live together, (7) No one else is claiming for Alex, (8) Alex lives at home normally with you, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is INELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. While Blake meets the basic child criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship) and you meet the claimant criteria (UK residency, responsibility, no other claimant), Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (the cumulative time limit) and you are not regularly spending money on Blake while in hospital. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation unless the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. Since you indicated you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs during the hospital stay, the exception does not apply.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is seeking Child Benefit for two children - Alex (6) and Blake (3). Both children meet the basic qualifying child criteria and the claimant meets residency and responsibility requirements. However, their eligibility differs due to their living situations. Alex lives at home normally and meets all requirements, making them eligible. Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, which triggers special rules. Child Benefit can continue beyond 12 weeks in hospital only if the claimant regularly spends money on the child, which the claimant is unable to do. Therefore, Alex is eligible but Blake is not eligible under current circumstances.

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

Alex meets all the eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), (6) You have responsibility for Alex as you live together, (7) No one else is claiming for Alex, (8) Alex lives at home normally with you, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is INELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. While Blake meets the basic child criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship) and you meet the claimant criteria (UK residency, responsibility, no other claimant), Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (the cumulative time limit) and you are not regularly spending money on Blake while in hospital. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation unless the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. Since you indicated you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs during the hospital stay, the exception does not apply.

Both children meet the basic qualifying child criteria and the claimant meets residency and responsibility requirements. However, their eligibility differs due to their living situations. Alex lives at home normally and meets all requirements, making them eligible. Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, which triggers special rules. Child Benefit can continue beyond 12 weeks in hospital only if the claimant regularly spends money on the child, which the claimant is unable to do. Therefore, Alex is eligible but Blake is not eligible under current circumstances.
